burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

burial.plot_notes: No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

career.notes: Succeeded brother-in-law Roger Newton

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's grandfather wrote a book Boston burned
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was Ann Pynchon, daughter of William Pynchon — who founded Springfield, Massachusetts, and whose 1650 book The Meritorious Price of Our Redemption became the first book publicly burned in New England. Thomas Hooker — grandson of the founder of Hartford — married the granddaughter of the man whose book the Puritans burned.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician in Hartford, son of Farmington's minister
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts in 1659 and trained as a physician in Hartford. He was the second son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington and great-grandson of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a widow whose husband drowned at sea
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Smith was the widow of Richard Lord II, lost at sea on November 5, 1685. Thomas married her between February and October 1686 — less than a year after she was widowed.

narrative_blocks.header: He managed his stepson's inheritance for seven years
narrative_blocks.body: When Thomas Hooker married the widow Mary Smith in 1686, he took charge of her first husband's Hartford estate. He administered it until 1693, when control passed to his stepson, young Richard Lord.

career.notes: Physician in Hartford. Also administered estate of Richard Lord II (wife's first husband) from 1686 until released Feb 8, 1693.

parents.mother_research_notes: Ann Pynchon — daughter of Y00004 Hon. William Pynchon, founder of Springfield MA.

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather founded Springfield — and was burned
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Ann Pynchon was the daughter of William Pynchon — who founded Springfield, Massachusetts, and whose 1650 book was the first to be publicly burned in New England. Mary carried one of colonial New England's most prominent lineages when she married Rev. Thomas Hooker's grandson.

narrative_blocks.header: First husband drowned — she ran the estate
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Richard Lord II, son of Hartford founder Richard Lord, was lost at sea on November 5, 1685, aged 49. Mary petitioned the court; in December 1686 she was awarded the personal estate, one-third of the real estate, and management of the Lord estate until her son Richard Lord III came of age. She managed it alone for seven years before marrying Thomas Hooker in 1686.

sources.notes: Samuel Hooker Jr. biography; confirms Plymouth birth, Hartford merchant activity

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Plymouth in his father's transition year
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in Plymouth, Massachusetts on May 29, 1661 — where his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had been preaching for several years. The family moved to Farmington that same year, where his father succeeded his brother-in-law Roger Newton as minister.

narrative_blocks.header: His house stood south of the burial ground
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hooker II had a fine residence at Farmington, next south of the old burial ground — later known as the Mix house — and ran a store for mercantile business in Hartford. He was one of the first of the Farmington Hookers to move from ministry into trade.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a wealthy sea captain's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1687 Samuel Hooker married Mehitabel Hamlin of Middletown, daughter of Capt. Giles Hamlin, a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant. Hooker kept a store and traded out of Hartford while living at Farmington.

career.notes: Mercantile business. Store and fine residence at Farmington.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard; later a fellow of the college. (Julius Gay, Farmington Historical Pamphlets)

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a sea captain
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Giles Hamlin was a noted and wealthy sea captain and merchant of Middletown, Connecticut. Her mother Esther Crow was the daughter of John Crow, merchant of Hartford — placing Mehitabel at the intersection of two of colonial Connecticut's most prominent merchant families.

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Middletown sea captain Giles Hamlin

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
Gravestone gives age 98. EH gives age 99.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: He heard his brother died
narrative_blocks.body: On hearing that his younger brother Giles had died at age 96, Samuel Hooker "fell back in his chair and expired" in Kensington on March 1, 1787, age 98. Their sister later wrote: "her brother Samuel died in 1787, ae. 99 — a long lived race." Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife was a granddaughter of the Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mercy Leete, whom he married in 1711, was the granddaughter of Governor William Leete of Connecticut Colony. His uncle James Hooker had also married a Leete granddaughter — Mary Leete, Mercy's first cousin through the same Governor. Two generations of the Hooker family married into the Leete gubernatorial line.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His father lived here, directly opposite the Giles Hooker house on Main Street.

narrative_blocks.header: His death at 96 killed his brother Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: On February 19, 1787, Giles Hooker died in Farmington at age 96. When the news reached his older brother Samuel — then age 98 in Kensington — Samuel "fell back in his chair and expired" on March 1, 1787. Two brothers, together accounting for nearly 195 years of life, died within ten days of each other.

narrative_blocks.header: The well stone on Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: He and Martha lived on the George Cowles place on Main Street, Farmington. In Edward Hooker's time (1909), a flat stone in the sidewalk just below the brow of the hill still covered the old well of the Giles Hooker house. Directly opposite, his father Rev. Samuel Hooker had lived in the Mix House — just south of the old burying ground.

narrative_blocks.header: Married widow Hannah Wells at fifty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He married widow Hannah Wells at age 57 in a late marriage with no issue. Edward Hooker gives no further entry on him beyond his marriage and death.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin John registered his Sue Squa deed

narrative_blocks.header: Four wives, five children — only one survived
narrative_blocks.body: He married four times across his 88 years. Of the five children produced across three of his four marriages, only one survived past childhood — and that son died unmarried at 27.

narrative_blocks.header: Sue Squa deed grantee on his cousin's land
narrative_blocks.body: On June 13, 1733 he received about three-quarters of an acre at Indian Neck, Farmington as part of the Sue Squa deed. His first cousin John Hooker — Hon. John Hooker, Probate Judge — served as witness, certifying official, and registrar of the same transaction.

cross_connections.display_label: Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.
Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife of Rev. Isaac Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Isaac Stiles of North Haven as his second wife. His first wife Keziah Taylor — daughter of the great Puritan poet Rev. Edward Taylor — had died in childbirth on December 4, 1727.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised Ezra Stiles, future Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: She raised her stepson Ezra Stiles from infancy after his birth mother Keziah Taylor's death. He became President of Yale (1778-1795) and one of the most important intellectuals of the American Founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten children with Stiles between 1729 and 1750
narrative_blocks.body: She and Isaac Stiles had ten children of their own between 1729 and 1750. Only Ezra was Keziah's son from the first marriage; all others were Esther's, carrying the Stiles-Hooker line forward through North Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name traveled five generations as Esther Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: The name 'Esther Hooker' became a heritable token across five generations of Stiles women — from her (1702) to her granddaughter (1760), and onward through Alice Stiles Mills (1845). Her name carried farther than her lineage.

bio.notes: In later years signed her name "Mabel"

cross_connections.display_label: His wife's sister married the Farmington minister he succeeded.
Their mothers were sisters — the Fenn family ran through both.
His father-in-law — patriarch of the Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Store at Farmington's most prominent corner
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker's store stood at the most prominent corner in Farmington — opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge. He died at 26, leaving only a daughter, Susannah, who was named in her grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker's will.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Fenn sisters married three prominent men
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Susanna Fenn was one of three Fenn sisters who married into prominent Connecticut families in the same generation. Susanna married him; her sister Martha married Samuel Newton; her sister Mary married William Leete Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-six with one infant daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He died in 1689 at age 26 — his will dated August 8 with inventory totaling £364-11-06. He left wife Susanna and an infant daughter Susannah; Susanna remarried John Blackleach within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Rev. Samuel Hooker, grandson of the founder
narrative_blocks.body: William was a son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington and a grandson of Thomas Hooker. His store stood across the road from his father's house.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow married the merchant John Blackleach
narrative_blocks.body: After William died in 1689, his widow Susanna Fenn married John Blackleach, a noted Hartford merchant. Their infant daughter Susannah carried the Hooker line forward.

career.notes: Store at the most prominent corner in Farmington, opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge.

sources.notes: Birth May 4, 1669; daughter of Benjamin Fenn and Susanna Wade

narrative_blocks.header: Her first husband died leaving her pregnant
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker died in 1689. Susanna gave birth to their daughter Susannah in June 1690 — born into a fatherless household. She then remarried John Blackleach, a noted Hartford merchant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna was a daughter of Benjamin Fenn by his second wife, Susanna Wade, and half-sister to Martha Fenn Newton. She married William Hooker of Farmington in the 1680s.

narrative_blocks.header: She remarried the merchant John Blackleach
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed when William Hooker died in 1689, Susanna married John Blackleach, a prominent Hartford merchant. She died about 1698.

death.cause: childbirth complications (died one month after daughter born Sept 3, 1709)

narrative_blocks.header: Born after her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father William Hooker died in 1689; she was born June 8, 1690, after his death. Her mother Susanna Fenn Blackleach died around 1698 — leaving Susannah an orphan at approximately eight years old. She was raised in the Farmington household through the end of the seventeenth century.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in childbirth at nineteen
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Susannah was born on September 3, 1709. On October 5, 1709 — barely one month later — she died in Stamford at age 19. The cause is almost certainly childbirth complications.

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.
cross_connections.display_label: helped govern Farmington alongside him for decades
married a descendant of both Hooker and Standley
served Hartford’s founding-family public life alongside him
was his wife’s sister and Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth’s grandmother
fathered four children who married four of his own
Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered
signed a Tunxis dispossession deed as his descendant’s in-law
shared his entanglement in Tunxis affairs, decades apart
was called with him one of Farmington’s two prominent men

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly in 1756
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

cross_connections.display_label: His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

death.cause: Year only; month/day approximate per project convention. Source: 'died 1761 aged 63.'

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons served the Revolution differently
narrative_blocks.body: Her eldest son Selah rose to General and was captured by the British in 1776; Noadiah served as Lieutenant; and Asahel, Yale-educated and ordained as a minister, died in 1775 at age 33 before the war's outcome was known. Three brothers from one Hooker-Hart household, three paths through the Revolution.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Hartford founding Hart family

open_questions.question: Elihu Burritt descent through this line
open_questions.notes: CLOSED: Elihu Burritt's Hooker descent through this line is NOT CONFIRMED. His connection to the tree is through KINSHIP, not descent: his grandfather Elijah Hinsdale (X01931) and Lydia Hinsdale Hart (I00034) were siblings — both children of Capt. John Hinsdale (X00039). This makes Burritt the first cousin once removed of Emma Hart Willard (H00597) and Almira Hart Phelps (H00598). The claim that Burritt descended from Thomas Hooker through Huldah Hart (H00280) could not be traced through available sources. He is documented in the tree as an orbit_notable (X01932) with the Hinsdale kinship connection noted.

sources.full: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, article on Hart family, New York Genealogical and Biographical Record
sources.author: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps (1793-1884), youngest daughter of Capt. Samuel Hart of Kensington, granddaughter of Lieut. Samuel Hart and Mary (Hooker) Hart

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Nathaniel Hart married Mary Hooker's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099) in 1719; Mary Hooker married I00029 in 1723.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Nathaniel Hart married Sarah Hooker's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099) in 1719; Sarah Hooker married I00030 in 1726.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Sarah Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Sarah Hooker married I00030 in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery (Ancient Burying Ground), Farmington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Farmington militia captain his entire life
narrative_blocks.body: He held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut militia and was known by that title for the rest of his life. He spent his entire life in Farmington — born there February 15, 1704/5 and dying there December 19, 1764, in his 60th year.

narrative_blocks.header: The big chair — militia saluted him sitting
narrative_blocks.body: In later years he became so corpulent he could not attend to business. On fine days a large chair was placed in the front yard near the door, where he sat smoking his pipe and visiting with passersby. When the local militia paraded, it was customary for them to march around by his house and salute him in the big chair.

narrative_blocks.header: Heir to the famous Farmington Hooker Elm
narrative_blocks.body: He was the son of Hon. John Hooker — the Farmington judge whose famous Hooker Elm gave the town its hospitality tradition. His grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker had ministered Farmington's church for thirty years; his great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker founded Hartford.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children with Capt. Asahel Strong Jr.
narrative_blocks.body: She married Capt. Asahel Strong Jr. on January 8, 1729 and bore him eight children — including Rev. Cyprian Strong of Chatham. Asahel was a grandson of Stephen Hart through his mother Margaret Hart, closing another loop in the Hart-Hooker network.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost three children in September 1736
narrative_blocks.body: In a single ten-day span — September 6 to 15, 1736 — three of her young children died: Lois and Ruth on the same day, then Elnathan nine days later. The cluster strongly suggests an epidemic moving through Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Second husband Solomon Whitman lived to 93
narrative_blocks.body: After Capt. Asahel Strong's death she married Solomon Whitman, who was Town Clerk at 92 and made a pair of shoes on his 90th birthday. He died at 93 from over-fatigue settling a fence dispute between two brothers.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

cross_connections.display_label: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies
was his father-in-law; son of Governor William Leete

narrative_blocks.header: He married the granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was the daughter of William Leete Jr. and the granddaughter of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut. James Hooker, born a Farmington pastor's son, married into one of the most distinguished families in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: First Probate Judge — presided over the Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first Probate Judge of the Guilford district and repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly, serving for a number of sessions as its presiding officer. EH notes he "became a very prominent man at Guilford."

narrative_blocks.header: His only son died at 21, name extinct
narrative_blocks.body: EH notes he "had but one son who died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name." That son was William, Yale class of 1722, who died at 21.

career.notes: First judge of the Guilford probate district; also a town magistrate.
Repeatedly represented Guilford; presiding officer for several sessions.

bio.title: Honorable
bio.bio_blurb: First probate judge of the Guilford district

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father was William Leete Jr., son of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and Colony of Connecticut. Her first cousin Mercy Leete (daughter of her uncle Andrew) also married a Hooker man. Two of the Governor's granddaughters, through different sons, both married into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a Connecticut governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was a granddaughter of Gov. William Leete, who governed both the New Haven and Connecticut colonies. She married Judge James Hooker in 1691 and they settled at Guilford.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford; actual grave uncertain. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband succeeded Daniel as Yale's second tutor
was her father-in-law

narrative_blocks.header: Married Yale's first B.A. graduate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 6, 1720 she married Rev. John Hart of East Guilford as his third wife. Hart had been Yale's first B.A. graduate (1703) and second tutor — succeeding her uncle Daniel Hooker, who had been Yale's first tutor.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of her six children survived to marry
narrative_blocks.body: With John Hart she had six children — four sons and two daughters. Two sons died young and infant Mary lived only three months. Sons Thomas (1723-1813) and Benjamin (1725-1804) carried the line, while daughter Sarah married three times — twice to Yale graduates.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Hooker married Thomas Smith of the New York Highlands. Her younger sister Mehitable married his brother, Rev. Dr. John Smith, joining the two families twice over.

enslaved_persons.name: Peleg
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Bequeathed in Sarah's will to daughter Anna Ruggles, then to grandson Nathaniel Ruggles Jr. if unsold at Anna's death. Probate inventory lists "Negro boy Peleg, 65 pounds."
enslaved_persons.source: Sarah Hooker Bartlett probate, Guilford, Connecticut

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Guilford CT; grave marker effaced. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Her will bequeathed an enslaved man named Peleg
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Bartlett's will, proved after her death in 1769, left to her daughter an enslaved man named Peleg, listed among the property of her Guilford estate.

burial.plot_notes: Alderbrook Cemetery, Guilford, New Haven County, Connecticut

education.notes: Yale graduate — class year not confirmed

narrative_blocks.header: Yale graduate, dead at twenty-one
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale around 1722 and died March 20, 1724 in Guilford at age 21, unmarried. Edward Hooker noted he 'died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name.'

narrative_blocks.header: The last Hooker of the Guilford line
narrative_blocks.body: He was the only son of Hon. James Hooker, the Probate Judge of Guilford. With his early death, the Hooker surname disappeared from Guilford within a single generation of arriving.

sources.short: Smith-Hooker Genealogy by Della Bishop, 1936, Ithaca NY

cross_connections.display_label: Mehitable Hooker Smith is the great-grandmother of Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with Rev. John Smith of Rye
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. John Smith of Rye, New York, and had twelve children between 1727 and 1750. The family lived in Westchester County during the years just before the Revolution turned that border country into a no-man's land.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandmother of Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandson Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran the Culper Ring for George Washington — the network that exposed Benedict Arnold's 1780 treason. Tallmadge was a classmate of Nathan Hale and a fellow Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Abigail at Washington's White Plains headquarters
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Abigail Smith married Jacob Purdy, whose Purchase, NY house served as Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains in 1776 and again in 1778. The Purdy descendants would carry the line through several Patriot–Loyalist conflicts.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Hooker married Rev. Dr. John Smith of Rye. Her elder sister Hannah had married his brother Thomas, so two Hooker sisters wed two Smith brothers.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Betrothed at 29 — passed before the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Hooker was betrothed to Mary Standley of Hartford when he died on April 29, 1698, at age 29. Seven months later — December 28, 1698 — his brother Nathaniel married her instead.

naming_inspiration.description: Likely named for Rev. Roger Newton (I00003), his father's predecessor and brother-in-law at Farmington.

bio.bio_blurb: Grandson of Thomas Hooker; died unmarried at twenty-nine

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter married her son, founding lines merged.

narrative_blocks.header: His bride had been promised to his brother
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Standley had been betrothed to Nathaniel's brother Roger Hooker, who died April 29, 1698. Seven months later — December 28, 1698 — Nathaniel married her instead.

narrative_blocks.header: His wedding gift was half of Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel married, his father-in-law Hon. Nathaniel Stanley gave him half his Hartford lot — extending from the Second Burial Ground to the little river. This became his place of business on the main street, just below the present Centre Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Two different Stanley families both married Hooker brothers
narrative_blocks.body: His brother John had married Abigail Standley of one Stanley family; Nathaniel married Mary Standley of a completely separate Stanley branch — Hon. Nathaniel Stanley of Hartford's line. Two unrelated families sharing the same name both converged on the Hooker brothers in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford's Colonial Assembly representative, dead at 40
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Hartford in the Connecticut Colonial Assembly for several years. He died November 21, 1711, at age 40, still in office.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married into the Talcott-Edwards line
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Hooker married Hon. Daniel Edwards in 1728 — whose mother was Mary Talcott, daughter of Lt.-Col. John Talcott. Their children thus descend from both Rev. Thomas Hooker and the founding Talcott line.

bio.bio_blurb: Hartford colonial assemblyman; grandson of Thomas Hooker

cross_connections.display_label: Both Standley women married Hooker brothers — different families.

narrative_blocks.header: Half a Hartford house lot came with marriage
narrative_blocks.body: When Mary Stanley married the Hartford merchant Nathaniel Hooker in 1698, her father gave the couple half his house lot, which ran from the Center Church burying-yard down to the Little River. Nathaniel served in the legislature until his death in 1711, after which she married another noted Hartford merchant, John Austin.

burial.plot_notes: Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Aged Maiden — died unmarried at sixty-five
narrative_blocks.body: She never married, living her entire life in Hartford. The CT Courant of January 14, 1765 noted her death simply: 'an aged Maiden, near Seventy Years old.'

narrative_blocks.header: Apoplexy in Hartford, January 1765
narrative_blocks.body: She died of apoplexy — a stroke — on Saturday, January 12, 1765, at age 65. She was baptized at the Second Church of Hartford on October 1, 1699 and lived all her years in the same town her great-grandfather had founded.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Hon. Daniel Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: Married Hon. Daniel Edwards — half-brother of Rev. Timothy Edwards, father of President Jonathan Edwards

narrative_blocks.header: Through the Edwards line
narrative_blocks.body: Through the Edwards line, Sarah Hooker connects the Hooker family to one of New England's most prominent theological dynasties

death.notes: Died young per EH; age ~2 estimated

narrative_blocks.header: Nathaniel died young
narrative_blocks.body: Nathaniel died young; the dates were not recorded.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings.
His wife's brother Matthew also married into the Hooker family line.
Both tied to separate Talcott branches in the same family generation.
Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Governor Talcott's daughter Eunice
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Talcott was the second daughter of Joseph Talcott, Governor of Connecticut from 1725 to 1741. Their marriage directly joined the two founding Hartford families — reinforced when Eunice's brother Matthew simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Named co-heir in the Governor's estate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 25, 1742, Nathaniel and Eunice appeared before the Court of Probate in Hartford to sign Governor Joseph Talcott's estate distribution as co-heirs. It was one of the final official acts binding the Hooker family to the colonial governor's wealth.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale man, militia captain, Assembly representative
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1729 and held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut Militia as early as 1734. He served as Hartford's representative in the General Assembly across three sessions, while running a substantial Hartford mercantile estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James partnered in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: His son James Hooker was a partner in the mercantile firm Hooker & Chaffee, which grew very successful during the Revolutionary War era. The firm continued the family's Hartford commercial standing into the next generation.

cross_connections.display_label: fathered the New Haven minister she married in 1698
was mother to the New Haven minister she married in 1698
her granddaughter, last of the line the heirloom apron passed through

notable.notable_blurb: Wife of Yale co-founder Rev. James Pierpont

artworks.artwork_blurb: Companion portrait to husband Rev. James Pierpont, 1711; Yale Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children in the house under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: Mary ran the New Haven parsonage through sixteen years of marriage and eight children, then presided over it as a widow for twenty-six more. The congregation had raised the house by voluntary subscription, and it stood for over a century.

narrative_blocks.header: Married James Pierpont the year her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father, the Farmington minister Samuel Hooker, died in November 1697; she married James Pierpont of New Haven the following year. The match carried her from one parsonage to the center of Connecticut’s emerging intellectual capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter’s courtship under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: It was Mary’s daughter Sarah whom Jonathan Edwards courted beneath the parsonage elms in 1726 — a match made in her widowed household that joined the Pierpont and Edwards lines and led on to college presidents and a vice president.

narrative_blocks.header: A son lost crossing the Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Samuel — Yale 1718, newly ordained at Lyme — drowned in March 1723 crossing the Connecticut River in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried where it was found.

narrative_blocks.header: The family that named an artist
narrative_blocks.body: The unidentified colonial painter who took her portrait around 1711 is known to art history only as the Pierpont Limner — christened for this family, whose likenesses anchor his surviving work. Her husband’s companion portrait hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: A silk apron may trace to her line
narrative_blocks.body: A rare 1730s embroidered silk apron at the Connecticut Historical Society descends through three generations of Marys beginning with her. Though scholars doubt she stitched it herself, her advantageous marriage made such a luxury possible for the daughters who followed.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mrs. James Pierpont (née Mary Hooker), 1711. Wikipedia Commons.

cross_connections.display_label: East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierson was first rector.
Pierpont built the institution Davenport had envisioned.
Descendant co-founded Skull and Bones at his Yale 130 years later.
coined the Ivy League name for colleges like Yale
his brother-in-law, a fellow Yale founder married to a Hooker sister

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Roxbury Latin School before Harvard.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait 1711 by Pierpont Limner; Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten ministers, forty books, one college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1700 Pierpont gathered ten ministers at Branford, each laying down volumes with the words “I give these books for the founding of a College in this Colony” — tradition credits James with six of the original forty. The charter he secured the next year became Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Two elms planted at the minister’s door
narrative_blocks.body: A poor farmer with nothing else to give carried two young elms on his shoulder and planted them before the new parsonage in 1686. Under their shade Jonathan Edwards courted Sarah Pierpont forty years on, and Whitefield preached at sixty. When the last elm finally came down, its sound trunk was sawed into blocks and scattered among the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brides in seven years
narrative_blocks.body: James buried two young wives in five years — Abigail, dead of a cold caught attending church in her wedding garments per the custom of the day, and Sarah, lost after childbirth. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker of Farmington, who bore eight children and outlived him by twenty-six years.

narrative_blocks.header: He drafted Connecticut’s church constitution
narrative_blocks.body: A leader of the 1708 Saybrook Synod, Pierpont is said to have drawn up the Articles of the Saybrook Platform — the system of discipline and fellowship that governed Connecticut’s churches for more than a century.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706 he was among five ministers who ordained Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — alongside Buckingham of Saybrook, Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Samuel Russell of Branford, and Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. All five names run through the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: The household that seeded three centuries
narrative_blocks.body: The New Haven parsonage became one of America’s great seedbeds: daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards, and the lines that followed carried two Timothy Dwights and Theodore Dwight Woolsey to Yale’s presidency, a vice president named Burr — and, further on, a financier named John Pierpont Morgan.

education.notes: Oldest boys’ school in the colonies, founded 1645.

career.notes: Ordained July 2 1685, ending an eleven-year pulpit vacancy; served until death.
The school became Yale College in 1718.

notable.notable_blurb: Son of Yale's founding trustee; Yale tutor 1722-24

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor 1722-24 — then apothecary
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale in 1718 and served as a tutor there 1722-24. He then established himself as an apothecary in Boston, continuing the business until his second marriage in 1754, when he moved to New Haven and carried on the same trade there until his death in 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: His three eldest sons all married sisters
narrative_blocks.body: The eldest three sons — Evelyn, Robert, and James — all married sisters, daughters of Charles Collins of Litchfield. The Collins sisters were daughters of Charles Collins of Litchfield.

narrative_blocks.header: Whitefield preached under his elm tree — twice
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. George Whitefield was twice the guest of James Pierpont — in October 1740 and again in 1745. On the latter occasion "he preached to an immense audience from a platform under the Elm tree, in front of Mr. Pierpont's house." (Pierpont occupied his father's residence at Elm and Temple Streets, New

narrative_blocks.header: Spent his last years chasing an English inheritance
narrative_blocks.body: The last years of his life were clouded by vain efforts to obtain recognition as the rightful heir to the titles and estates of the Pierrepont family in England. (His son Evelyn carried this further — His son Evelyn pursued this claim further.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister married Edwards — the Aaron Burr legacy
narrative_blocks.body: His sister Sarah Pierpont Edwards married Jonathan Edwards, the Great Awakening theologian. Their daughter Esther married Rev. Aaron Burr Sr., and their son Aaron Burr became the 3rd Vice President of the United States and killed Alexander Hamilton in a duel in 1804. The Pierpont siblings split into two of the most consequential American lineages.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale tutor, then apothecary in Boston and New Haven

career.notes: Occupied his father’s residence at Elm and Temple Streets.

bio.bio_blurb: Lyme’s young pastor, drowned crossing the Connecticut

career.notes: Ordained Dec 10, 1722; drowned three months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned three months after his ordination
narrative_blocks.body: Newly ordained pastor of Lyme, Samuel drowned on March 15, 1723, crossing the Connecticut River above Saybrook Ferry in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried at the island’s westerly end, where his gravestone still tells the whole story.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.
Her daughter's marriage to Talcott converged founding lines.

institutions.institution_blurb: Father Rev. James Pierpont Sr. and father-in-law Rev. Noadiah Russell were both founding trustees of Yale in the same 1701 charter.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father and father-in-law both founded Yale
narrative_blocks.body: She was the daughter of Rev. James Pierpont Sr., one of the ten founding trustees who chartered Yale College on October 16, 1701. She married Rev. William Russell, son of Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — who was also one of those ten founding trustees.

narrative_blocks.header: Four founding families converge in her children's generation
narrative_blocks.body: Through her parents she carried the Hooker line and the Pierpont-Yale line. Through marriage she joined the Russell-Yale line. Her daughter Mary Russell married Matthew Talcott — adding the Talcott founding line to the convergence her children embodied.

narrative_blocks.header: A hidden Hooker-Hamlin connection in her husband's family
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. William Russell's mother Mary Hamlin was the sister of Mehitable Hamlin, who married Samuel Hooker Jr. — a brother of Mary Pierpont's own mother. This made William Russell's aunt the wife of Mary's uncle, drawing a second bond between the families before their own marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Hartford's founder — nine children
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather was Rev. Thomas Hooker, who led the 1636 migration from Cambridge that founded Hartford. She settled in Middletown with her husband and bore nine children: Mary, Esther, William, Samuel, Noadiah, James, Sarah, Mehitable, and Hannah.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter Mary married Matthew Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Mary Russell married Matthew Talcott of Hartford — the marriage that created the Russell-Talcott convergence. Their children were both Hooker and Talcott descendants, connecting two of colonial Connecticut's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: She may have stitched the family apron
narrative_blocks.body: Of the three Marys linked to a surviving 1730s silk apron, she is one of two likeliest embroiderers, depending on which attended a Boston finishing school and when. The apron’s Chinese silk and French metallic thread mark the family’s reach into global trade.

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of the Russell nine at Middletown

cross_connections.display_label: Married the sister of his own sister's husband, both Pierpont-Russell siblings.

bio.bio_blurb: North Haven farmer with twelve children

military_service.notes: FindAGrave: Lieut Joseph Pierpont; North Haven farmer.

narrative_blocks.header: Double sibling marriage joined Pierponts and Russells
narrative_blocks.body: He married Hannah Russell around 1725 while his sister Mary married Hannah's brother William the same year. Two siblings on each side, married into a single family in one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His marriage joined two Yale-founding families
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. James Pierpont was a Yale founding trustee in 1701. His wife's father Rev. Noadiah Russell was another.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children at his North Haven farm
narrative_blocks.body: He farmed land about ten miles northeast of New Haven through the 1730s and 1740s. Several of his twelve children died in infancy, including the first James in 1727.

narrative_blocks.header: Benjamin died in his first year
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 18, 1706, Benjamin died that December. His parents gave the name to a son born ten months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on Virgin Gorda, far from New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: The second Benjamin, named for the brother who died in infancy, graduated from Yale and died unmarried in 1733 on the island of Virgin Gorda in the West Indies — the family record does not say what carried him there.

sources.short: Life of Rev. Jonathan Edwards by Sereno Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Princeton Cemetery, Princeton, New Jersey — buried next to Jonathan Edwards

cross_connections.display_label: Died trying to bring him home from Princeton at age two.
Died trying to bring her home from Princeton at age four.
Her North Haven nephew — wealthiest man in town in 1786

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Pierpont Edwards (1710-1758), wife of Jonathan Edwards.
bio.bio_blurb: Subject of Edwards’s famous meditation at thirteen

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards wrote about her when she was twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Edwards composed a private spiritual meditation about a 'young lady in New Haven who is beloved of that Great Being who made and rules the world.' She married him five years later, at seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought home her orphaned Burr grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: After Jonathan's death she traveled south in autumn 1758 to retrieve four-year-old Sarah Burr and two-year-old Aaron Burr Jr. She fell sick with dysentery in Philadelphia before she could complete the journey.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly six months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan died of smallpox-inoculation complications on March 22, 1758, weeks into the Princeton presidency. Sarah died of dysentery on October 2, 1758, at forty-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Made her will the day she died
narrative_blocks.body: On October 2, 1758, in Philadelphia, she dictated her last instructions hours before dysentery took her. The two Burr grandchildren she had been escorting were placed with Dr. William Shippen.

narrative_blocks.header: Her portrait hangs in a Yale dining hall
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband were both painted in oils, traditionally attributed to John Smybert. The portraits were bequeathed to Yale in 1939 and now hang in Jonathan Edwards College.

burial.plot_notes: Center Church on the Green Churchyard, New Haven, Connecticut

bio.bio_blurb: Root of the Brooklyn Pierrepont line

narrative_blocks.header: The family genealogy runs through him
narrative_blocks.body: The youngest son died at twenty-nine, yet the Pierrepont genealogy devotes its central chapter to his line — which led to the Brooklyn Pierreponts. His widow Lydia Hemingway remarried to Theophilus Morgan.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at Farmington at about age ten
narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Hooker was born at Farmington in 1675, one of Rev. Samuel Hooker's eleven children. He died there in 1686, about ten years old.

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

notable.notable_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate, 1703
notable.primary_url_label: Historic Buildings CT — Dr. Daniel Hooker House 1725

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor, B.A. graduate in 1703
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first tutor appointed at the Collegiate School (Yale) by first rector Abraham Pierson Jr. — Yale's earliest faculty member. He received his Yale B.A. in 1703, alongside Rev. John Hart who was the first student to graduate.

narrative_blocks.header: Surgeon of the Canada expedition, 1711
narrative_blocks.body: He served as surgeon of the Massachusetts expedition against Canada in 1711 — the final major military operation of Queen Anne's War. He then returned to Wethersfield to practice medicine and law.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined his father's pulpit — chose medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, Farmington invited Daniel to succeed his father. He declined the pulpit and chose medicine — studying law alongside it, not to practice but to help patients make their wills.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington-born man with a college education
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Harvard in 1700, then enrolled at the new Collegiate School in Saybrook to receive its B.A. in 1703. He was the first Farmington-born man to obtain a college education.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited and divided Thomas Hooker's West Hartford farm
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited Thomas Hooker's farm in West Hartford and in 1728 deeded it to his sons. The land had been part of the original Hooker grant — passing through three generations from founder to surgeon.

institutions.institution_blurb: First Yale tutor 1701-1704; first B.A. graduate 1703.

cross_connections.display_label: He tutored under Pierson — Yale's first rector.
His niece married John Hart, his Yale successor
His successor as Yale tutor — second graduate
was his father-in-law

career.notes: First tutor of Yale Collegiate School. Hired by rector Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127). Resigned in favor of Rev. John Hart (I00137).
Settled Wethersfield after leaving Yale. Also studied law to help patients make wills — no evidence he practiced law professionally.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard 1700. First Farmington-born man to receive a college education.

Received degree at Yale's first commencement 1703. EH says he was "the first B.A. of Yale" but also that Rev. John Hart (I00137) was "nominally the second, but really the first actual student to graduate." Daniel was the tutor/faculty; Hart was the first student B.A. Conflict flagged — see research_notes.

bio.title: Doctor
bio.bio_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate; later a Wethersfield physician

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

sources.notes: Wethersfield Village Cemetery; carved by William Stanclift

burial.plot_notes: Marker type: gravestone. Original marker survives. Carved by William Stanclift. One of the older surviving carved gravestones in the region.

death.notes: Estimated: birth + ~60yr colonial average; no death record

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery; no stone marks the grave (tradition). SOURCE: EH/FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: The most accomplished lady Norwalk had ever seen
narrative_blocks.body: She was nicknamed 'Dame Buckingham' and described in family tradition as 'the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.' She lived at Norwalk with her husband Rev. Stephen Buckingham from their 1703 marriage until her death in 1759.

narrative_blocks.header: No stone marks her grave — tradition knows
narrative_blocks.body: She died about March 26, 1759 in Norwalk, age 77. No marker survives at her grave; the location is preserved only in family tradition and her husband's burial records.

narrative_blocks.header: Her stepfather's son became her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After her father Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, her mother Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook. Thomas Buckingham was the father of Rev. Stephen Buckingham — whom Sarah herself married. After Thomas died in 1709, Mary came to Sarah and Stephen at Norwalk and lived with them until her death in 1712.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law

bio.bio_blurb: "Dame Buckingham" of Norwalk; granddaughter of Thomas Hooker

parents.father_research_notes: X01001 married I00006 Mary Willet (widow of H00007 Samuel Hooker) in 1703. So Stephen's father became the stepfather of Stephen's wife Sarah's mother.

burial.plot_notes: East Norwalk Historical Cemetery, Norwalk, Fairfield County CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: First name in Yale's first Triennial Catalogue
narrative_blocks.body: At Yale's first commencement in 1702, Stephen received his second academic degree — the first awarded by the new Collegiate School. His name appears first in the Triennial Catalogue of Yale College, making him the college's first officially recorded graduate, despite having taken his first degree at Harvard in 1693.

narrative_blocks.header: His will left everything to his wife Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen's will, dated January 1, 1745/46 and proved February 18 in Fairfield, was simple and complete: he left everything to his wife Sarah. They had been married nearly sixty years.

narrative_blocks.header: His nephew died visiting at 24
narrative_blocks.body: Jedidiah Buckingham — Stephen's nephew — visited him in Norwalk in 1720. He had recently left his pulpit in Newark, New Jersey. He died at Stephen's house March 28, 1720, aged 24, and was buried in Norwalk.

narrative_blocks.header: Cleared of charges — then pushed out anyway
narrative_blocks.body: After thirty years at Norwalk, Buckingham was charged with intemperance. A Consociation investigated and found no fault in his conduct. The town then found a letter they could construe as a resignation and accepted it before he could object.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's mother married his own father
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen Buckingham married Sarah Hooker of Farmington. Her widowed mother, Mary Willet, later married his father, Rev. Thomas Buckingham, making his mother-in-law his stepmother as well.

cross_connections.display_label: his brother-in-law, who likewise married a Hooker and helped found Yale

education.notes: Received his second degree at Yale's first commencement; his name stands first in the college's Triennial Catalogue. Later a member of the Yale Corporation.

bio.bio_blurb: Norwalk pastor; among Yale’s first graduates

notable.notable_blurb: Yale founder; hosted the Saybrook Platform 1708

married his son Stephen

narrative_blocks.header: Hosted the Saybrook Platform in his own house
narrative_blocks.body: On September 9, 1708, the Connecticut Synod assembled at his Saybrook home: twelve ministers, four laymen, eight of the twelve being Yale Trustees. The result was the Saybrook Platform — the document that defined Connecticut Congregationalism for the next century, a carefully negotiated middle way between Massachusetts and Presbyterian practice.

narrative_blocks.header: One of twelve original Trustees of Yale College
narrative_blocks.body: He was among the twelve ministers who signed Yale's founding documents in 1701. He died April 1, 1709 — "second in age among the Trustees, and for forty-three years pastor of Saybrook." His son Stephen received Yale's first academic degree at its first commencement.

narrative_blocks.header: His son had married his new wife's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: When Thomas Buckingham married the widow Mary Willet Hooker in 1703, his son Stephen was already the husband of her daughter Sarah. The father and son had married a mother and her daughter.

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Cemetery, Old Saybrook, Middlesex County CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Founding trustee of Yale; minister at Saybrook

notable.notable_blurb: First Mayor of New York City, 1665

narrative_blocks.header: From Plymouth Pilgrim to New York's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City by Governor Richard Nicolls in 1665, shortly after the English took the city from the Dutch. He had joined the English expedition against New Amsterdam the previous year alongside Governor Winthrop of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded Miles Standish as Plymouth's military captain
narrative_blocks.body: A trusted Plymouth Colony leader, Willet succeeded the legendary Miles Standish as Captain of the Plymouth military company in 1648. He had arrived in Plymouth on the ship Lion in 1632, associated with the Leyden congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: He bought Attleboro from Massasoit's son
narrative_blocks.body: As a Plymouth trader, Willett negotiated the Rehoboth North Purchase, acquiring the lands that became Attleboro and North Attleboro. He bought them from Wamsutta, a son of the Wampanoag sachem Massasoit.

narrative_blocks.header: He brokered New Amsterdam's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: In 1664 Willett joined the English expedition under Richard Nicolls and helped arrange the peaceful surrender of Dutch New Amsterdam. When the city was renamed New York, he became its first mayor the next year.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Plymouth's Major John Brown
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Browne was a daughter of Major John Brown, a leading citizen of Plymouth Colony. In 1636 she married the trader Thomas Willett, who would become New York's first mayor.

narrative_blocks.header: She bore fourteen children across two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Browne and Thomas Willett had fourteen children over more than twenty years at Plymouth. She died early in 1670, and her husband remarried the following year.
